Target mass error includes weight and
experimental set-up using a detector with a given
efficiency curve in a given geometry; it depends on the
evaluation of the monitor counts and the beam charge
Geometry: difference in the positioning of the
sample with regard to the neutron source and the
detector; within one angular distribution this
uncertainty is uncorrelated, but not for the reference
The Monte Carlo simulation corrects for flux
attenuation,multiple scattering and finite sample size.
consists of the combined statistical errors,

Manfred Drosg (2018-01-09): From the high energy
tailing of all neutron spectra it can be concluded that
the 14.1 MeV neutron source was contaminated with
higher energy neutrons. The origin of these neutrons is
traced back to angular straggling of the deuterons of
the beam lowering the emission angle of the neutrons

differential cross section over both the energy of
the emitted neutrons and the solid angle was done in
two steps: first, the cross section of the highest
energy peak was converted into the center-of-mass
system, and then the remainder was integrated over
the energy and the solid angle in the laboratory

In most cases the worse time resolution at higher
energies did not allow the separation of scattering
from the low-lying excited states. Therefore, Table
VII gives the differential laboratory cross section
of the highest energy peak rather than elastic cross
sections. In 6Li case, inelastic cross sections of
the first excited state at 2.186 MeV was subtracted.

These data contain extrapolations to zero
neutron energy. To account for events below the
neutron detection bias, the following simple
procedure was set forth: It was assumed that the
spectrum below the cutoff of 0.35 MeV was in the
average as intense as the spectrum between 0.35 MeV
and 2.0 MeV. We assigned an error of 50% to this
